expanded_description: Formalizing geometric similarity as a composition of rigid motions and dilations, connecting transformational definitions to classical similarity criteria and proportional reasoning

core_concept.principle: Two figures are similar if and only if one can be mapped onto the other through a sequence of rigid motions (translations, rotations, reflections) followed by a dilation
core_concept.explanation: Similarity preserves shape but allows size changes. Rigid motions align the figures in position and orientation, while the dilation scales all linear dimensions by a constant factor k. This transformational definition explains why similar figures have congruent corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides. The scale factor k equals the ratio of any corresponding lengths: $k = \frac{\text{Image Length}}{\text{Preimage Length}}$.

image.alt: Triangle undergoing rotation then dilation to map onto a larger similar triangle
image.caption: Figure 1: Similarity = Rigid Motion (aligns shape) + Dilation (scales size)

sections.introduction: Rather than relying solely on angle/side measurements, similarity can be rigorously defined through transformations. This approach unifies congruence and scaling under one mathematical framework.

key_concept.definition: Figure A is similar to Figure B if there exists a composition $D \circ R$ where $R$ is a rigid motion and $D$ is a dilation that maps A exactly onto B
key_concept.context: The order of operations can vary (rigid motion first or dilation first), but placing the dilation last is standard in coordinate geometry because it simplifies algebraic verification

image.alt: Step-by-step visual: original shape -> rotated position -> dilated final shape
image.caption: Figure 2: Rigid motions preserve size; dilations change size; together they preserve shape

principles.statement: If a similarity transformation maps Figure A to Figure B, then all corresponding angles are congruent and all corresponding side lengths are proportional
principles.application: Use this to prove similarity without measuring every angle: find the transformation sequence that maps one onto the other
principles.example: To show $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$, translate A to D, rotate to align AB with DE, then dilate by $k = \frac{DE}{AB}$
principles.analogy: Like taking a photo, rotating it, then zooming in—the subject looks identical, just positioned differently and larger

worked_examples.title: Mapping Similar Triangles with Transformations
worked_examples.problem: Triangle $PQR$ has vertices $P(0,0)$, $Q(2,1)$, $R(1,3)$. Triangle $STU$ has vertices $S(4,2)$, $T(8,4)$, $U(6,8)$. Show that $\triangle PQR \sim \triangle STU$ by identifying the similarity transformation.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare corresponding sides using distance formula

First dilate $\triangle PQR$ by $k=2$ about origin: $P(0,0) \to (0,0)$, $Q(2,1) \to (4,2) = S$, $R(1,3) \to (2,6)$. Then translate by $\langle 0, 2 \rangle$ to match $U(6,8)$... Wait, check coordinates: $U(6,8)$ vs dilated $R'(2,6)$. Actually, apply dilation by 2 about $(0,0)$ gives $R'(2,6)$. To get $U(6,8)$, we need translation $\langle 4, 2 \rangle$. Check $S$: $(4,2) + (4,2) = (8,4) = T$. Check $S$ original: $(0,0)+(4,2)=(4,2)=S$. Perfect.

worked_examples.final_answer: $\triangle PQR \sim \triangle STU$ via dilation by $k=2$ followed by translation $\langle 4,2 \rangle$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Constructing explicit similarity transformation from coordinates

sections.section_title: Connecting Transformations to AA, SAS, and SSS Criteria
sections.introduction: The classic similarity criteria are actually shortcuts that guarantee a valid similarity transformation exists. Understanding this connection deepens geometric reasoning.

categories.description: If two angles of one triangle are congruent to two angles of another, the triangles are similar
categories.detailed_mechanism: Congruent angles mean the third angles must also match. Rigid motions align two angles, and the parallel postulate forces the sides to be proportional, enabling a dilation to complete the mapping

examples.explanation: A line parallel to one side of a triangle creates a smaller similar triangle via AA. Rigid motion aligns vertices; dilation scales the parallel segment proportionally

image.alt: Triangle with parallel segment creating AA similar sub-triangle
image.caption: Figure 3: AA criterion guarantees proportional sides without measuring them

categories.description: If two sides are proportional and the included angle is congruent, the triangles are similar
categories.detailed_mechanism: Rigid motions align the congruent included angles. The proportional sides mean a dilation centered at the angle vertex will scale one side to match the other, forcing the third side to scale proportionally by properties of parallel lines

examples.explanation: Given two sides in ratio 1:3 and matching angle, dilating the smaller triangle by k=3 about the angle vertex maps it exactly onto the larger

image.alt: Two triangles sharing an angle with proportional adjacent sides
image.caption: Figure 4: SAS similarity reduces to aligning angle + scaling sides

categories.description: If all three corresponding sides are proportional, the triangles are similar
categories.detailed_mechanism: Apply dilation first to match one side length. The other two sides automatically scale by the same k. Rigid motions then align the matching side and rotate/reflect until all vertices coincide, proving angles must be congruent

examples.explanation: If all dimensions of a drawing scale by the same factor from the original, the shapes are guaranteed identical in form

image.alt: Triangles with side length ratios labeled showing constant proportion
image.caption: Figure 5: SSS similarity ensures complete shape preservation through proportional scaling

worked_examples.title: Proving Similarity Using SAS Criterion
worked_examples.problem: In $\triangle ABC$ and $\triangle DEF$, $AB = 4$, $AC = 6$, $m\angle A = 50°$. In $\triangle DEF$, $DE = 10$, $DF = 15$, $m\angle D = 50°$. Prove similarity and find the scale factor from $\triangle ABC$ to $\triangle DEF$.

step_by_step.explanation: Compare ratios of sides enclosing the congruent angle
Proportional sides + congruent included angle implies similarity
Translate A to D, rotate to align sides, dilate by k=2.5 about D

worked_examples.final_answer: $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ by SAS Similarity. Scale factor $k = 2.5$.
worked_examples.concept_applied: SAS similarity guarantees existence of rigid motion + dilation mapping

sections.section_title: Properties Preserved and Changed by Similarity
sections.introduction: Understanding which properties remain invariant under similarity transformations versus those that scale is crucial for problem-solving and real-world applications.

key_concept.definition: Similarity transformations preserve angles, parallelism, collinearity, and midpoint relationships, while scaling all linear measurements by k and areas by k²
key_concept.context: This distinction explains why maps preserve directions but distort distances, and why architectural models look identical to buildings but weigh far less

image.alt: Side-by-side comparison showing preserved angles and scaled side lengths
image.caption: Figure 6: Shape properties preserved; size properties scaled

process_flow.description: Find ratio of any pair of corresponding lengths
Multiply original length, perimeter, median, altitude, radius by k
Confirm angles unchanged, parallel lines remain parallel, slopes match
process_flow.organization: Use preserved properties to set up proportions or prove geometric relationships

principles.statement: For similar figures with scale factor k: $\frac{P_1}{P_2} = k$ and $\frac{A_1}{A_2} = k^2$
principles.application: Never assume area scales linearly with side lengths; always square the scale factor for area problems
principles.example: If a garden is scaled up by k=3 for a model, its perimeter triples, but its area becomes 9 times larger
principles.analogy: Like tiling a floor: doubling the room's side length requires 4 times as many tiles, not 2

worked_examples.title: Perimeter and Area Scaling in Similar Polygons
worked_examples.problem: Two similar regular pentagons have corresponding side lengths 6 cm and 18 cm. If the smaller pentagon has area 62 cm² and perimeter 30 cm, find the area and perimeter of the larger pentagon.
worked_examples.final_answer: Larger perimeter = 90 cm; Larger area = 558 cm²
worked_examples.concept_applied: Linear vs quadratic scaling in similar figures

sections.introduction: Similarity transformations provide powerful tools for coordinate geometry proofs and practical scaling problems in engineering, cartography, and design.

key_concept.definition: In the coordinate plane, similarity can be proven by showing constant distance ratios between all vertex pairs, or by explicitly constructing the rigid motion and dilation sequence
key_concept.context: Coordinate proofs combine distance formula, slope calculations, and transformation algebra to rigorously establish similarity without physical measurement

image.alt: Two triangles on coordinate grid with distance calculations showing constant ratio
image.caption: Figure 7: Algebraic verification of similarity using coordinate distances

worked_examples.title: Coordinate Geometry Similarity Proof
worked_examples.problem: Triangle $ABC$ has vertices $A(1,1)$, $B(4,1)$, $C(1,5)$. Triangle $DEF$ has vertices $D(2,3)$, $E(8,3)$, $F(2,11)$. Prove $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ using coordinate methods and find the transformation.

step_by_step.explanation: Check if constant scale factor exists
Translate A to D, dilate by 2

worked_examples.final_answer: $\triangle ABC \sim \triangle DEF$ by SSS with $k=2$. Transformation: Translate $\langle 1,2 \rangle$, then dilate by 2 about D.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Coordinate distance ratios prove similarity and reveal transformation sequence

worked_examples.title: Scale Drawing Application
worked_examples.problem: An architect's blueprint uses a scale where 1 inch represents 4 feet. A rectangular room on the blueprint measures 3.5 inches by 5 inches. What are the actual dimensions? If the blueprint area is 17.5 in², what is the actual floor area?

step_by_step.explanation: Convert units consistently: 4 ft = 48 in, so $k = 48$

worked_examples.final_answer: Actual dimensions: 14 ft × 20 ft; Actual area: 280 ft²
worked_examples.concept_applied: Real-world scale factor application with linear and area scaling

summary: Geometric similarity is formally defined as a composition of rigid motions and a dilation. Two figures are similar if one can be mapped onto the other through translations, rotations, or reflections (which preserve size and shape) followed by a dilation (which scales size while preserving shape). This transformational definition explains why similar figures always have congruent corresponding angles and proportional corresponding sides, with scale factor $k$ equal to the ratio of any corresponding lengths. The classic similarity criteria—AA, SAS, and SSS—are geometric shortcuts that guarantee such a transformation sequence exists. When figures are similar with scale factor $k$, linear measurements (side lengths, perimeter, altitudes, medians) scale by $k$, while areas scale by $k^2$. Angles, parallelism, collinearity, and midpoint relationships remain invariant. In coordinate geometry, similarity is proven by showing constant distance ratios between all vertex pairs or by explicitly constructing the transformation sequence. Real-world applications include scale drawings, architectural blueprints, map reading, model building, and engineering design, where understanding the precise relationship between shape preservation and size scaling enables accurate planning, material estimation, and spatial reasoning.
